exemplar.title: To what extent is the method of measuring fractal dimension applicable to real life Spatial Planning problems?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent is the method of measuring fractal dimension applicable to real life Spatial Planning problems?

subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Clear narrative with introduction, modelling, evaluation and conclusion sections
Effective use of headings, tables and graphs to guide the reader
Mostly concise with relevant material supporting the research question
weaknesses: Occasional repetition and minor digressions reduce conciseness
Inconsistent citation format in the bibliography and OCR artifacts in tables
Long bibliography paragraph and under-referenced appendix hinder perfect organization
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration is coherent and logically developed, with a well-defined structure and mostly relevant content. Minor organizational and conciseness issues prevent it from reaching the highest band, so a 3 is appropriate.

strengths: Consistent use of key symbols and standard notation throughout
Multiple representations (derivations, log-log graphs, data tables) that complement each other
Logical progression of mathematical ideas with clear explanations
weaknesses: Minor slips in log notation (base undeclared) and algebraic transformations
Axis label and caption mismatches on at least one graph
Omission of an explicit algebraic step linking slope to fractal dimension
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student communicates mathematically with good clarity, using appropriate notation and effective representations. A few small errors prevent a perfect score, so 3 marks accurately reflect the quality.

strengths: Strong personal context and choice of a novel local island problem
Clear demonstration of independent thinking in error-bound derivation
Good creativity using Google Earth overlays and practical constraints
weaknesses: Methodology remains largely standard without deeper methodological innovation
No exploration of alternative fractal models or advanced statistical checks
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work shows significant independent thinking and a clear personal approach with creativity, but falls short of outstanding innovation, placing it securely at 2 marks.

strengths: Meaningful reflection on sources of error and method limitations throughout
Thoughtful analysis comparing modeled results with known dimensions
Clear evaluation of outcomes with suggested improvements
weaknesses: Reflection is descriptive rather than critically in-depth
Evaluation lacks rigorous quantification of proposed improvements and future implications
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Reflection and analysis are present and thoughtful, addressing outcomes and limitations. They are not yet deeply critical or comprehensive, justifying 2 marks.

strengths: Mathematics is applied at every stageâfrom derivation to data fitting and error estimation
Effective use of logarithmic transformations and real-world data comparisons
Well-justified methodological choice and sensitivity considerations across two case studies
weaknesses: Minor sign and exponent errors in derivations that require correction
Limited mathematical breadth beyond logarithms and linear regression
Statistical validation (e.g., residual analysis) is not fully explored
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student uses mathematics in a comprehensive, expert manner, integrating derivations, data analysis and error estimation seamlessly into the investigation. Although small technical errors remain, the depth and consistency of application merit 5 marks to reflect its overall strength.

strandSelections.reasoning:
Explanations usually proceed step-by-step: definition â derivation â data â graph â slope â dimension.  This provides a good logical thread and clarity for the reader.

There are isolated mis-statements (e.g., negative slope equated to 1+D) but these are corrected in the numeric result, showing minor inconsistency rather than serious breakdown.  Argumentation remains understandable.

Hence the work demonstrates âgood logical structure and clear communicationâ, earning 3 marks.

Derivations are correct and calculations lead to plausible values (1.19, 1.21, 1.15).  The student correctly interprets gradients and carries out logarithmic conversions.

Minor inaccuracies occur: sign inconsistency when relating slope to 1-D, rounding of log values to 4 s.f. without discussing effect on gradient, and use of only four points per graph.  These indicate good but not thorough understanding and produce small numerical errors.

Consequently the strand meets âgood understanding with minor errorsâ, earning 3 marks.

The student employs multiple representations: descriptive text, algebraic derivations, three log-log graphs, and three data tables.  Each representation supports the others and helps the reader see patterns.

Choice of only four data points per graph limits statistical strength, but the visualisations are appropriate and clearly labelled.  Additional representations (e.g., residual plots) would be needed for the highest band.

Thus use of representations is effective but not âhighly effectiveâ, fitting the 3-mark level.

The exploration follows a clear narrative: introduction, background theory, modelling, checks with two test cases, main case study, evaluation and conclusion.  Ideas build logically â the student explains fractal dimension, derives a working formula, validates the procedure on Australia/Great Britain, and finally applies it to Petrovsky Island.  Although occasional digressions and minor algebra slips occur, the overall line of argument can be followed without confusion.

Transitions between sections are evident (âModelling of the fractal dimensionâ, âEvaluationâ, âConclusionâ), and each section contributes to answering the research question.  Some passages are slightly repetitive (e.g., re-explaining Coastline Paradox), preventing the work from reaching the exemplary band.

Consequently the work is judged coherent and logically developed, but not at an âexcellentâ level.

Using Google Earth, custom segment overlays and error estimations reveals creativity and initiative beyond minimal requirements.  Still, the exploration stays within a single, quite simple mathematical model and does not extend to, for example, statistical regression checking or alternative fractal measures.

Hence it demonstrates a âgood level of creativity and initiativeâ rather than exceptional, warranting 2 marks.

The analysis of results (comparison with known dimensions, computation of average error 0.05) is thoughtful.  The student questions the practicality of the method and links mathematical findings back to spatial-planning uses.

Nevertheless, analysis is based on very small data sets and does not employ statistical tools or rigorous justification for chosen error bounds; insight remains moderate.

Thus the strand fits the âthoughtful analysisâ (2 marks) rather than âdeep and insightfulâ.

Outcomes are evaluated against Google Earth measurements and published fractal dimensions, and the student discusses how improvements (more segment lengths, alternative methods, peer checking) could enhance accuracy.  Future applicability to medicine or image compression is briefly mentioned.

Evaluation is clear but not comprehensive; quantitative justification of the proposed improvements is limited.

Hence the strand aligns with the 2-mark descriptor âclear evaluation of outcomes and implicationsâ.

The student reflects meaningfully on error sources (eye placement, scale, human-made structures) and on why the Hausdorff method may not suit large-scale planning.  Strengths and limitations of the work are identified, with suggestions for more segment lengths and peer verification.

Reflection is integrated throughout but remains largely descriptive; it rarely probes underlying mathematical assumptions or wider implications in depth.  This satisfies the âmeaningful reflection throughoutâ descriptor (2 marks).

Key symbols (\(D, L, l, k, \log\)) are introduced and used consistently, and standard notation for logarithms and exponents is applied.  Minor slips such as writing âlogâ without base declaration or the sign error in â(1+D)â occur but do not persist.

Mathematical sentences are generally complete and punctuated, showing mostly consistent use of language and notation.

Therefore the communication reaches the âmostly consistentâ standard rather than flawless excellence.

The personal context (interest in spatial planning and Saint-Petersburg) frames the exploration.  Choices of example islands, acknowledgement of practical constraints, and self-generated data all indicate a clear personal approach.

Yet the analysis generally follows standard procedures without highly distinctive or innovative twists.  Consequently engagement meets the descriptor for 2 marks.

At 16 pages the essay stays within IB limits and most material is pertinent to fractal dimension and coastline length.  Some redundancies remain (duplicate definitions, repeated quotations, lengthy personal anecdotes) and there are a few irrelevant screenshots, so the piece is not fully âhighly conciseâ.

Nevertheless the majority of content directly supports the modelling or the evaluation.  Repetition does not dominate and irrelevant digressions are limited.

Hence the strand satisfies âconcise with mostly relevant contentâ, meriting 3 marks.

Logarithmic manipulation, linearisation, slope extraction and error estimation are all directly relevant to determining fractal dimension and coastline length, fully supporting the research question.  Mathematics is used at each stage of the investigation rather than appearing as isolated fragments.

The range of mathematics is not extensive, but its application is effective, matching the 4-mark ârelevant mathematics used effectivelyâ band.

Selecting a local, newly developed island and designing a measurement procedure shows significant independent thinking beyond textbook examples.  The student also derives error bounds and critiques applicability.

However, most of the mathematical approach (Hausdorff method, log-log plotting) is lifted directly from existing resources; innovations in methodology or deeper mathematical investigation are limited.

This corresponds to âsignificant evidence of independent thinkingâ (2 marks) rather than âoutstandingâ.

Headings, sub-headings and page numbers are used effectively; tables and graphs are embedded near the relevant text.  The reader can locate definitions, data and analysis quickly.  Introduction, body and conclusion are distinct, though the conclusion blends evaluation and reflection rather than separating them formally â still acceptable for âclear structureâ.

Minor organisational weaknesses include a very long bibliography paragraph inside the main text and an appendix referenced only once, but these do not seriously hinder navigation.

Overall the exploration is âwell organised with clear structureâ, matching the 3-mark descriptor.

Logarithms, exponent rules, gradient interpretation and proportional reasoning are all within the AA SL syllabus and are applied correctly to a real-world context.  The work demonstrates mastery of these SL-level tools; no steps rely on mathematics below the course level.

Because the mathematics remains relatively elementary and does not extend far beyond standard content, award 4 marks (âfully commensurate with course levelâ) rather than 5 or 6.

annotations.comment: The research question is clearly stated and frames the investigation effectively, guiding the reader to the aim and scope of the exploration.
The student effectively uses personal context and background to demonstrate genuine interest, enhancing engagement and grounding the mathematical exploration in real-world experience.
The statement of the aim ends abruptly and lacks clarity on the subsequent methodological steps; complete this section to outline the specific procedures and scope.
Condense the background section by focusing on key definitions and remove redundant explanations to improve conciseness and relevance.
Including a visual representation of a Koch snowflake with random perturbations effectively illustrates the concept of self-similarity and supports the theoretical description.
Clarify the general derivation of fractal dimension by explicitly stating the relationship $$N=(1/s)^D$$ before applying it to specific examples for greater mathematical rigour.
The clear section headings and logical structure guide the reader through the modelling process, enhancing coherence of the mathematical exposition.
In the equation $$N = (1/s)^D$$, ensure consistent notation by using parentheses correctly and defining each variable immediately to avoid potential confusion.
The step $$\log(N)=\log\bigl((1/s)^D\bigr)$$ should explicitly apply the exponent rule to yield $$\log(N)=D\log(1/s)$$, improving clarity of the log transformation.
Expand the comparison between the Box Counting and Hausdorff methods by outlining key advantages and limitations of each, supporting the choice of method for this investigation.
The justification for selecting the Hausdorff method over Box Counting is well-articulated, showing thoughtful methodological choice for realâworld coastline measurement.
The formula appears as $$L = k l^{1-L}$$ but should correctly read $$L = k\,l^{1-D}$$; this exponent error must be corrected for consistent derivation.
Combine the two separate tables of segment lengths and map data into one comprehensive table to improve organization and reduce the need for crossâreferencing.
The table presenting segment lengths, counts, coastline lengths, and their logarithms is clear and aids in understanding the dataset used for linear regression.
The caption for the Australia graph references \(\log(I)\) instead of \(\log(l)\); ensure axis labels and captions consistently match the variables plotted.
The student insightfully acknowledges practical limitations of eyeâplacement and map scale, demonstrating meaningful reflection on sources of error.
Include the explicit algebraic step showing how $$m=(1-D)$$ leads to $$D=1-m$$ to reinforce the logical progression in solving for the fractal dimension.
When comparing modelled and theoretical dimensions, consider quantifying the uncertainty from rounding and graphâfitting to present a more rigorous evaluation of error.
Averaging the errors from two case studies to estimate overall uncertainty shows thoughtful consideration of variability in the methodâs accuracy.
Beyond visual placement and scale, discuss how map projection distortion and coastline dynamics (e.g., tides) could further influence the error estimate.
Relying on a single Google Earth measurement without sensitivity analysis risks overconfidence; consider varying segment orientations or multiple measurements.
The table header contains OCR artifacts (e.g., âHumberâ and extraneous LaTeX code) that obscure meaning; clean up labels for readability.
The Petrovsky island logâlog plot is well labeled and neatly presented, facilitating clear visual interpretation of the linear relationship.
The derivation equates the slope as $$-0.1498=(1 + D)$$, but it should be $$-0.1498=(1 - D)$$; correct this to maintain consistency with the linear model.
Comparing the Petrovsky result with known dimensions of Great Britain and Norway demonstrates thoughtful contextualization and critical evaluation of outcomes.
Discuss the practical implications of a fractal dimension range from 1.10 to 1.20âwhat does this variation mean for spatial planning accuracy?
Outlining specific improvement strategiesâmore segment lengths, peer checks, alternative methodsâshows substantial reflection on enhancing methodological accuracy.
The conclusion asserts irrelevance of the method too broadly; refine to acknowledge scenarios where fractal dimension measurement could still inform spatial planning.
The answer section is lengthy and repetitive; condense to a concise summary of key findings, limitations, and direct answer to the research question.
Identifying future research areas in medicine and construction demonstrates creativity and shows ongoing personal engagement with the topic beyond geography.
The reflective narrative on learning to use Excel and Google Earth evidences deep engagement with mathematical practice and enhances the reflection criterion.
Map entries in the bibliography use varying formats; adopt a consistent citation style (e.g., APA/MLA) to improve presentation and professionalism.
